title: Be Strong and Courageous — God Is With You
scripture_text: Have I not commanded you? Be strong and courageous. Do not be afraid; do not be discouraged, for the Lord your God will be with you wherever you go.
reflection: Courage is not the absence of fear — it is the decision to move forward in spite of it. Joshua received this command at a moment of immense pressure, standing at the threshold of the promised land with the weight of an entire nation on his shoulders. God's response was not a promise of an easy road but a sure anchor: 'I will be with you.' When courage is rooted not in self-confidence but in the presence of God, it becomes something far deeper than bravado. It becomes trust.
body:
Find a comfortable position and let your body settle. Close your eyes if that feels right, or simply lower your gaze. Take a slow breath in through your nose, and let it out gently through your mouth. There is nowhere else to be right now. There is nothing to prove in this moment. You are simply here, and that is enough.

I want to talk with you today about courage. Not the kind of courage that makes the news or earns medals — though that courage is real and honorable. I want to talk about the everyday kind. The kind that shows up when you have to have a hard conversation you have been avoiding. The kind that shows up when you have to try again after failing. The kind that shows up when the path forward is not clear and you have to take a step anyway.

You may be feeling some of that weight today. Maybe there is a decision looming and you do not feel ready for it. Maybe there is a change coming and you are not sure you can handle it. Maybe there is a voice inside you that keeps saying: you are not enough, you are not prepared, you are not strong enough for this. I want to speak directly to that voice today.

God said to Joshua — a man who had seen miracles, who had walked through the wilderness with Moses, who had witnessed the Red Sea part — God said to him: be strong and courageous. Not because Joshua was naturally fearless. Not because he had it all figured out. But because he needed to hear the most important part of that sentence: for the Lord your God will be with you wherever you go.

Courage is not the absence of God in your life. Courage is the presence of God expressed as action. When you move forward not because you are certain of victory, but because you trust the One who goes before you — that is courage. When you take the next step not because you can see the whole staircase, but because you believe the One holding your hand knows the way — that is courage.

Right now, I want you to bring to mind something that has been making you afraid. Not the big overwhelming things — just one thing. One fear, one worry, one challenge that has been sitting on your chest. Hold it gently, and then receive this truth: the same God who was with Joshua, who parted the Red Sea, who raised Jesus from the dead — that God is with you right now. He has not forgotten you. He has not left you. He is with you in the fear, and he is with you beyond it.

Be strong. That word in the original language means to hold firm, to grip tightly to what is true. Be courageous. That word means to take action, to move forward even when your hands are shaking. Do not be afraid. Do not lose heart. God goes before you, beside you, and behind you. You are not walking this alone.

What is the one brave step you need to take this week? It does not have to be a big dramatic leap. Sometimes the bravest thing you can do is make one small phone call, send one honest message, start one difficult conversation, or simply decide to stop running from something God is asking you to face. Ask him right now: Lord, what is the brave next step? And then trust that he will show you.

You are not alone in this. You never were. Be strong. Be courageous. And know that God is with you — today, tomorrow, and every day that follows.
prayer: Father, you know the fears I carry today. You know the weight I have been trying to manage on my own. Thank you for your promise: you are with me. Help me to be strong and courageous, not in my own strength but in yours. Give me the wisdom to know the next step, and the grace to take it. In Jesus' name, Amen.

title: Loved Before You Could Love Back
scripture_text: We love because he loved us first.
reflection: Love is the central word of the Christian faith, and this single verse cuts to its heart: we love not because we are naturally lovable, but because God loved us first. This reorders everything. It means love is not something we must conjure from our own depleted reserves — it is something we receive, rest in, and then pass on. The life marked by love is not the life of someone who has figured out how to love perfectly, but the life of someone who keeps returning to the source of love and letting it overflow.
body:
Let your breathing slow. Let the noise of the day settle. There is nothing required of you right now except to be present, and to receive. Take a slow breath in, hold it gently, and let it go. In this moment, you are held. You are known. You are loved — not for what you do, not for what you have accomplished, not for what you look like on the outside, but simply because you are his.

We live in a world that tells us we have to earn love. Perform well enough, be attractive enough, be smart enough, be productive enough, be good enough — and then, maybe, you will be worthy of love. That is the lie underneath so much of our exhaustion. We are running on a treadmill trying to earn something that has already been given to us freely.

We love because he loved us first. That is not a command — it is a statement of fact. God did not wait for us to clean ourselves up. He did not hold his love back until we proved ourselves worthy. Romans chapter five puts it plainly: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us. That is the order of grace. He loved first. He loved when it cost him everything. He loved you at your worst, and he loves you still.

What does it mean to rest in that love? It means you do not have to perform to access it. You do not have to be perfect to draw near to God — you simply come as you are, with all your rough edges and unfinished places, and you let his love cover the rest. You do not earn it by being better. You receive it by being honest.

And here is the part that changes everything: when you truly understand that you are loved without condition, something inside you shifts. The love you have received becomes the love you have to give. Not because you are strong enough on your own, but because the love of God, received and trusted, overflows. You find yourself loving people you did not think you could love. Forgiving people you thought were beyond forgiveness. Reaching out when your instinct was to pull back.

1 John says it beautifully: we love because he loved us first. That word 'because' makes all the difference. Our love is not the source — it is the overflow. We do not love from a place of emptiness trying to fill itself. We love from a place of fullness that cannot help but pour out.

Think about one person in your life who has been difficult to love. Perhaps it is someone who has hurt you. Perhaps it is someone whose choices baffle you. Perhaps it is someone you have simply grown tired of. Bring them to mind right now. And then remember: that person is loved by the same God who loves you. That person is included in the grace you have received. You do not have to earn the right to love them — you simply get to participate in the love God already has for them.

Let us make this our prayer not just today but in the days ahead: Lord, fill me with your love until it overflows. Help me to love not from my own strength, which runs out, but from your love, which never runs dry. Teach me to receive your love more deeply so that I can give it more freely. And when I fail at loving as you call me to — because I will — remind me that your love is not finished with me yet.

You are loved. You have always been loved. You will always be loved. Rest in that truth today, and let it change the way you live.
prayer: Father, thank you for loving me first. I receive your love today as a gift, not as a wage. Fill me with it until it spills over onto everyone around me — especially the people I find hardest to love. Teach me to give your love away, and help me to rest in the knowledge that I am yours. In Jesus' name, Amen.
